abstract: This paper provides a comprehensive comparative analysis of DeepSeek-R1 and OpenAI’s ChatGPT, examining the architectural, methodological, and operational differences between these two prominent Large Language Models (LLMs). While ChatGPT follows a proprietary, dense transformer-based approach refined through Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), DeepSeek-R1 introduces a cost-efficient alternative utilizing a Mixture of Experts (MoE) architecture and Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA). Notably, DeepSeek-R1 emphasizes a multi-stage training process heavily grounded in rule-based reinforcement learning, which bolsters its performance in specialized reasoning, mathematical, and technical tasks. In contrast, ChatGPT maintains a competitive edge in general-purpose conversation, linguistic fluency, and creative adaptability. This study evaluates both models across several dimensions, including benchmark performance, transparency, ethical considerations, and data privacy. By synthesizing their respective strengths and limitations, the paper highlights the trade-offs between open-source customization and closed-source user experience. Ultimately, the findings suggest that while neither model dominates every domain, the choice between them is dictated by specific organizational needs, such as computational budget, transparency requirements, and the complexity of the target reasoning tasks. The paper concludes by addressing broader AI challenges, including data quality, algorithmic "black box" problems, and the evolving regulatory landscape.
